title: Price Theory and Inflation Indices
focus: Combines microeconomic price elasticity concepts with the methodology and comparison of PPI, WPI, and CPI.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 033]
5. GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT 5.1. THE ECONOMICS OF PRICE THE ECONOMICS OF PRICE: SUPPLY & DEMAND RELATIONSHIPS * Scenario A: Demand Increases - Demand curve shifts right (D1 to D2). Price increases, Quantity increases. * Scenario B: Demand Decreases - Demand curve shifts left (D1 to D2). Price decreases, Quantity decreases. * Scenario C: Supply Increases - Supply curve shifts right (S1 to S2). Price decreases, Quantity increases. * Scenario D: Supply Decreases - Supply curve shifts left (S1 to S2). Price increases, Quantity decreases. * Legend: Blue Lines & Arrows = Demand Shifts; Orange Lines & Arrows = Supply Shifts; Up Arrow = Increase; Down Arrow = Decrease. 5.2. INFLATION & PRICE ELASTICITY HOW INFLATION & GOOD TYPE AFFECT PRICE ELASTICITY: The 'Wallet Squeeze' & Its Impact on Consumer Choices. * Concept: Inflation (Reduced Real Income) leads to the 'Wallet Squeeze' where consumers become generally more price sensitive (Elasticity Increases) for non-essentials. * Impact by Good Type: 1. A. NECESSITY (Inelastic): Normal Behavior: Food, Fuel, Medicine (Buy regardless of price). Behavior During Inflation: Brand Switch (Premium to Cheaper Alternative) to save budget for other goods. 2. B. LUXURY / DISCRETIONARY (Elastic): Normal Behavior: Designer Clothes, Vacations (Sensitive to price). Behavior During Inflation: Hyper-Elastic; sales plummet as these are the first to be cut. 3. C. DURABLE GOODS (Postponable): Normal Behavior: Cars, Appliances (Purchase decision). Behavior During Inflation: Delayed; consumers make old goods last and postpone purchases. 4. D. INFERIOR GOODS (Trading Down): Normal Behavior: Generic Spam, Instant Noodles (Bought when income is low). Behavior During Inflation: Demand Increases; consumers switch to these from expensive options. * Legend: Steep Curve = Inelastic; Flat Curve = Elastic; Arrow = Shift in Demand/Behavior.

[Page 034]
5.3. PRODUCER PRICE INDEX
Why in the News
A new 18-member working group, led by Niti Aayog member Ramesh Chand, to examine the proposed Producers’ Price Index (PPI) and suggest a roadmap for switching from the WPI to the PPI.
About PPI
• It measures the average change in the price a producer receives for his goods/services sold in the domestic market/ exports.
• Two types:
o Output PPI: When goods/ services leave the place of production
o Input PPI: When goods/ services enter the production process.
Need to replace WPI
• WPI has inbuilt bias due to double/ multiple counting of same product.
